metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ensure 35d2's documentation consistency test (or 6777's test) explicitly scans docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md and any other new guidance files for hardcoded personal sign-off identities.
evidence: child 35d2 AC: 'A documentation consistency test or equivalent deterministic check prevents reintroducing a globally hardcoded personal sign-off identity' — scoped to AGENTS.md, .agents/rules/rebar.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, and 'generated/imported guidance'

child 6777 AC: 'A lightweight documentation test verifies that ChatGPT guidance remains linked and does not contradict canonical Rebar or Gerrit rules' — no explicit mention of identity-neutrality constraint
child 6777 file_impact lists docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md as a new file; this file will contain Gerrit/commit guidance that could include a sign-off example
child 35d2 file_impact does not list docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md (the file does not yet exist at time of 35d2 authoring)
container pairing: parent + 1aa8-cdde-4ff6-40d0/35d2-8cd6-161c-4e7f/6777-5581-1ef1-4b7b/b24d-a840-ea8a-4a54/fe4c-315c-eab7-43b1
findings.finding: Child 6777 creates a new documentation file (docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md) but neither 6777's acceptance criteria nor its documentation consistency test is explicitly scoped to prevent reintroducing a hardcoded Joe Oakhart bot sign-off identity — the exact pattern that child 35d2's remediation and consistency test are designed to eradicate. The new file is outside the textual scope of 35d2's consistency check as written.
scenarios: A developer implements 6777 after 35d2 is merged and, while drafting ChatGPT commit guidance, copies a pre-remediation AGENTS.md example that includes 'Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart <joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com>'; 35d2's consistency test does not scan the new file and the regression is not caught
The 6777 documentation test checks for link validity and Gerrit rule consistency but not identity hardcoding, leaving the specific DCO regression class uncovered for the new file
findings.suggested_fix: In child 35d2's consistency test, expand the file glob to include all files under docs/ and templates/ (not only the currently listed files). Alternatively, add an explicit AC in 6777 stating that the new guidance file must not contain a hardcoded personal DCO identity and must be covered by 35d2's consistency check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document the outcome-type handoff contract between the verifier (1aa8) and the plan-review orchestrator (b24d), including how the orchestrator handles verifier-produced indeterminate/contract-failure outcomes.
evidence: child 1aa8-cdde-4ff6-40d0 AC: 'Empty-after-recovery and unrecoverable responses produce a distinct explicit indeterminate or contract-failure outcome'
child b24d-a840-ea8a-4a54 AC: 'A remaining incomplete hierarchy cannot receive an ordinary clean pass'
Neither ticket's `deps` list references the other (both show `deps: []` in ticket data)

b24d file_impact includes `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py`; 1aa8 file_impact includes `src/rebar/llm/verify_models.py` — adjacent pipeline stages where orchestrator likely consumes verifier output
No child specifies the protocol for orchestrator behavior upon receiving a verifier-indeterminate outcome
container pairing: parent + 1aa8-cdde-4ff6-40d0/35d2-8cd6-161c-4e7f/6777-5581-1ef1-4b7b/b24d-a840-ea8a-4a54/fe4c-315c-eab7-43b1
findings.finding: No child owns the integration contract between the new verifier indeterminate/contract-failure outcome (1aa8) and the plan-review orchestrator's handling of it (b24d). Both children introduce new explicit failure outcome types in adjacent pipeline stages, but neither child names the other as a dependency or specifies how the orchestrator should behave when it receives a verifier-produced indeterminate outcome rather than a clean or recovered result.
scenarios: An LLM verification call returns an unrecoverable response (1aa8 logic fires, emits contract-failure outcome); the plan-review orchestrator (b24d) receives this as verifier output — since neither child specifies this handoff, the orchestrator may silently treat the contract-failure as an ordinary non-pass, log it inconsistently, or apply b24d's indeterminate path incorrectly

The two children are implemented independently in sequence, and their new outcome-type enumerations diverge (e.g., 1aa8 uses 'contract_failure' while b24d expects 'indeterminate'), causing a type mismatch at the boundary
findings.suggested_fix: Add a shared outcome-type definition (e.g., a typed enum in `src/rebar/llm/`) referenced by both children. Either add a cross-dep in b24d's ticket pointing to 1aa8, or add a combined integration test that exercises the full path from a verifier contract-failure through to the orchestrator's indeterminate verdict.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'semantically empty result' concretely in AC3 (e.g., 'a result object whose `findings` and `affirmations` fields are both empty/absent, or whose entire payload is null/empty-string') so the guard condition is implementable and in-session verifiable.
evidence: Plan text: 'no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome'
ABSENCE: no definition, enumeration, or example of what constitutes a 'semantically empty' result anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: 'Semantically empty result' in AC3 is undefined: an executor cannot determine what specific outcome shape (null, empty string, empty JSON arrays, no findings + no affirmations, etc.) constitutes a rejected empty result, blocking both implementation and in-session verification.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome'
scenarios: An agent implementing the guard condition cannot write a predicate without knowing the exact shape that triggers rejection.
A reviewer verifying the AC cannot determine pass/fail without a concrete test case for 'semantically empty'.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome' with a concrete predicate, e.g.: 'a review outcome is rejected if it contains zero findings AND zero affirmations, or if the parsed payload is null, an empty string, or a structurally empty object — regardless of provider.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC4 what 'demonstrably irrelevant' means operationally (e.g., 'the loaded hierarchy has no open children, no blocking dependencies, and no cross-ticket references cited in the plan under review') and clarify what distinguishes an 'ordinary clean pass' from other pass outcomes.
evidence: Plan text: 'Plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely, unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant'
ABSENCE: no protocol, heuristic, or enumerated criteria for assessing 'demonstrably irrelevant'
ABSENCE: 'ordinary clean pass' is not distinguished from other pass types anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: AC4's escape clause 'unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant' is undefined: the executor has no criterion for what counts as 'demonstrably irrelevant' or who/what makes that determination, making the AC unverifiable in-session and creating an unbounded subjective gate.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant'
scenarios: An executor implementing the hierarchy-loading guard must decide when to allow the escape hatch — without a standard, every incomplete load could be argued irrelevant.
A reviewer verifying AC4 cannot confirm pass/fail because 'demonstrably irrelevant' admits any interpretation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific file or document that constitutes 'public transition guidance' in AC2 (e.g., `docs/transitions.md` or the Rebar contributor guide) so the executor knows exactly where to write the new text.
evidence: Plan text: 'Public transition guidance states that force bypasses all gates, including future gates'
ABSENCE: no file path, document name, or section reference identifying where this guidance lives
findings.finding: AC2 does not specify which file or document 'public transition guidance' refers to, leaving the executor to discover or infer the target artifact.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'Public transition guidance states that force bypasses all gates…'
scenarios: An executor unsure whether to update an existing doc, create a new one, or amend a README may produce the change in the wrong location.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out mechanism (e.g., a feature flag or phased rollout) for the AC3 behavioral tightening so that if the empty-result rejection logic produces false positives, reviews can be unblocked without a full redeploy.
evidence: 'no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome' — this converts a previously permissive gate into a hard rejection
No mention of a back-out plan, feature flag, or phased rollout if the new rejection logic causes unexpected failures in existing workflows
findings.finding: AC3 tightens existing verification behavior (rejecting semantically empty outcomes) without acknowledging the expand-contract sequence or providing a rollback path for pipelines or workflows that currently tolerate empty results.
scenarios: A partial LLM provider response that was previously accepted as a graceful degradation now hard-fails a review, breaking a pipeline with no clear revert path.
An edge-case provider (e.g., ChatGPT in connector-limited mode) returns a minimal but non-empty result that the new logic misclassifies as empty, causing a permanent gate failure.
findings.suggested_fix: State an expand-contract sequence: first log-and-warn on empty results (observability phase), then promote to hard reject after a defined soak period; document the flag or config knob that reverts to warn-only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'semantically empty' with a small cross-provider fixture corpus (e.g., 3–5 real provider responses spanning empty, terse-valid, and full outcomes) and add a RED test that the detection logic must pass before the AC is considered met.
evidence: 'no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome' — 'semantically empty' is undefined and not derived from measurement
'flexible provider-independent parsing' implies behavior varies across providers, making a single threshold hard to validate by reasoning alone
No RED fixture, example corpus, or pilot measurement is mentioned to calibrate the empty/non-empty boundary
findings.finding: AC3's central heuristic — distinguishing a 'semantically empty' result from a 'legitimately minimal but valid' review outcome — is asserted without a spike, fixture, or cross-provider sample to establish where the boundary lies.
scenarios: An LLM returns a one-sentence finding list that is technically non-empty but carries no signal; the gate passes it when it should not.
A terse-but-valid provider response is misclassified as empty, causing a false rejection that erodes trust in the gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Create a fixture file with labeled examples (empty / borderline / valid) drawn from actual provider outputs in the target environments (including ChatGPT connector-limited mode) and write a parameterized test that the classifier must pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'semantically empty' precisely (e.g., required non-null fields, minimum finding-count, or schema version) and expose the definition as a configurable or schema-driven rule rather than an implicit code constant.
evidence: 'Verification preserves flexible provider-independent parsing and self-healing, but no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome.'
The plan establishes a non-acceptance rule without defining what constitutes 'semantically empty' or noting that the definition is configurable or versioned.
AC3 simultaneously calls for provider-independent flexibility (suggesting the parsing layer will evolve) while introducing a hardcoded accept/reject threshold — a tension the plan does not resolve.
findings.finding: AC3 introduces a new invariant ('no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome') as a pipeline gate in the verification stage, but 'semantically empty' is not defined, nor is the threshold specified as configurable. What constitutes an empty outcome may differ by LLM provider, output schema version, or review type, making this rule likely to require tuning — yet the plan treats it as a hardcoded binary gate.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome'
scenarios: A future LLM provider whose structured output format returns minimal but non-empty valid JSON (e.g., a review with only a 'status' field and no findings) could be incorrectly rejected or incorrectly accepted depending on how 'semantically empty' is later implemented, with no configuration surface to adjust without a code change.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a named concept (e.g., `minimum_valid_review` schema or a documented required-fields list) to the relevant design doc or AGENTS.md, and note that the threshold is configurable per review type. Reference this definition from AC3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section documenting the new pipeline-gate contracts introduced by AC3 and AC4, including the definition of 'semantically empty', the 'demonstrably irrelevant' adjudication rule for AC4, and the rationale for each gate.
evidence: 'no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome' — new gate in verification pipeline.
'Plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely, unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant.' — new gate in plan-review pipeline.
No mention of an ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design document anywhere in the plan.
The phrase 'demonstrably irrelevant' in AC4 encodes a non-trivial adjudication rule that is not documented or attributed to any decision record.
findings.finding: AC3 and AC4 together introduce two new pipeline-stage contracts (empty-outcome rejection in verification; incomplete-hierarchy gate in plan review) that constitute novel architectural decisions. The plan names no ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design-doc update to capture these decisions, leaving future maintainers without a record of the intent, the edge cases considered, or the rationale for the exact contract boundaries.
scenarios: A maintainer adding a new ticket-hierarchy loader in the future will not know whether their loader is expected to satisfy the 'demonstrably irrelevant' exemption, because the rationale and boundary conditions for AC4's gate are not recorded anywhere the plan specifies.
findings.suggested_fix: Create a short ADR (or a named section in AGENTS.md) titled e.g. 'Review-validity gates (2026-07-19)' covering: (1) what makes a verification result semantically empty, (2) what evidence satisfies 'demonstrably irrelevant' for missing hierarchy context, and (3) how each gate is enforced and tested.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each behavioral gate tightened (AC3, AC4), add an explicit acknowledgment of known callers/workflows that relied on the old behavior and document the migration or exception path (e.g., fallback on LLM timeout vs. empty result for AC3; 'demonstrably irrelevant' escape-hatch operationalization for AC4).
evidence: AC3 changes a pass/fail boundary for LLM verification that previously accepted empty outcomes
AC4 adds a new gate that previously did not exist
No mention of migration, deprecation notice, or affected caller inventory for either behavioral change
Plan summary frames these as 'restoring optionality' and 'making verification resilient' but does not inventory what breaks
findings.finding: The plan tightens two behavioral gates — AC3 (rejecting semantically empty verification results) and AC4 (blocking plan reviews with incomplete hierarchy context) — without acknowledging existing workflows or callers that currently depend on the old, more permissive behavior and may break silently.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome') and AC4 ('plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely')
scenarios: An automated pipeline that produces empty LLM verification output (e.g., model timeout, rate-limit, network issue) will now hard-fail under AC3 with no fallback documented
A plan-review workflow that deliberately omits parent-ticket context (treating it as irrelevant) will be blocked by AC4 with no documented escape hatch or appeal path
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC3 narrative, distinguish between a truly empty result (model returned nothing useful) and a transient error (timeout, parse failure) — specify that transient errors must surface an error state rather than an empty-pass, and document any retry or fallback. For AC4, define 'demonstrably irrelevant' with at least one concrete example so reviewers can apply the exception without ambiguity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete validation mechanism across the five ACs — for example: a worked example session demonstrating the ChatGPT workflow (AC5), a before/after trace showing empty-result rejection (AC3), or a test scenario that exercises the incomplete-hierarchy gate (AC4).
evidence: AC1–AC5 each describe a static end-state ('guidance uses…', 'guidance states…', 'no result can be accepted…') with no specified test scenario, review checklist execution, or metric
F4(d) requires 'at least one criterion carries a concrete validation mechanism (before/after workflow comparison, an operational metric target, dogfooding)'
ABSENCE: no mention of a smoke test, integration run, or example session demonstrating the fix works
findings.finding: None of the five acceptance criteria include a concrete validation mechanism (before/after workflow comparison, operational metric, or dogfooding run); they describe desired end-states but not how correctness will be verified post-implementation.
scenarios: An implementer ships the guidance docs and behavioral gate changes; without a validation step, a subtle mis-wording or incomplete gate implementation goes undetected until a real incident
For AC5 (ChatGPT workflow guidance), there is no dogfooding requirement — guidance could be internally inconsistent or reference unavailable features without being caught
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Validation' subsection to the plan listing: (a) a sample ChatGPT session transcript demonstrating AC5 guidance in action; (b) a unit/integration test or manual trace proving AC3 rejects an empty-body LLM response; (c) a contrived plan-review run with a missing parent ticket that the gate blocks per AC4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism for AC#4: state whether incomplete hierarchy loading will (a) add an explicit coverage-gap signal the verdict checks before issuing a clean pass, (b) fail-closed for container tickets (and reconcile with the documented fail-open invariant), or another approach — and note how the 'demonstrably irrelevant' exception will be determined.
evidence: AC text: 'Plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely, unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant.'
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:175-177: 'except Exception: … logger.warning("could not list children of %s; reviewing without", … listed = []' — explicit fail-open
docs/plan-review-gate.md asymmetric-error invariants table: 'DET floor: fail-open — a check that cannot run abstains (recorded coverage) and is treated as PASS'
The plan's Summary and body sections name no mechanism for how to detect incomplete loading and prevent a clean pass
findings.finding: AC#4 ('plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely') requires a code change with non-trivial design implications, but the plan provides no mechanism. The current code (`_assemble_context_uncached` at orchestrator.py:175-177) deliberately catches child-enumeration exceptions and continues with an empty child list — a documented fail-open design decision (plan-review-gate.md's asymmetric-error invariants table). Making a clean pass impossible after incomplete loading requires either (a) converting this fail-open behavior to fail-closed for containers (which contradicts the invariant table) or (b) adding a coverage signal that the verdict machinery must check. The plan states the desired outcome but is silent on which mechanism it will use, leaving the implementer to resolve a real design conflict independently.
scenarios: A container ticket's children fail to enumerate (store read error, network partition, snapshot mismatch). Current code silently continues and reviews the parent as if childless. AC#4 says this must not yield a clean pass — but without a specified mechanism, the implementer must independently choose between fail-closed (breaks the fail-open invariant) or coverage-signal (requires verdict machinery changes not described in the plan).
The 'unless demonstrably irrelevant' qualifier in AC#4 requires a relevance judgment — the plan provides no approach for making that judgment programmatically.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a mechanism statement to AC#4, e.g.: 'When child enumeration fails during a container review, record the failure as a `coverage_gap` signal; the verdict logic treats a container review with an unresolved `coverage_gap` as INDETERMINATE rather than a clean PASS (preserving the fail-open invariant while making the incompleteness observable).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC#3 by distinguishing between (a) a missing/unparseable structured payload (already guarded by `StructuredOutputError` — state if this is what AC#3 targets and what, if anything, will change) and (b) a zero-finding list (a valid clean-plan outcome that must NOT be blocked), and state the precise mechanism if a new safeguard is being added.
evidence: AC text: 'Verification preserves flexible provider-independent parsing and self-healing, but no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome.'

src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py and docs/llm-framework.md: 'No silent empty reviews. If the agent returns no structured payload … the runner raises StructuredOutputError rather than returning zero findings.'

An empty findings list (zero findings) IS a valid, expected plan-review outcome for a clean plan — the plan-review finder prompt explicitly states 'A clean chunk returns an empty findings list (and affirms the criteria it passed) — that is expected and good.'
The plan body provides no description of what code or behavior will change to satisfy this AC
findings.finding: AC#3 ('no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome') does not describe what change will be made, and conflates two distinct states that require different treatments: (1) a parse-failed/missing structured payload (already handled by the existing `StructuredOutputError` mechanism) and (2) a zero-finding list (a valid, expected outcome for a clean plan). If the plan intends to add a new safeguard beyond what exists, it must state what 'semantically empty' means precisely and what code will change. If it only means documenting an existing safeguard, the AC should say so.
scenarios: Implementer interprets 'semantically empty' as zero findings and adds a guard that rejects clean reviews — incorrectly blocking legitimate zero-finding verdicts.
Implementer interprets it as the already-existing StructuredOutputError guard and makes no code change — AC is satisfied vacuously, adding no value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for ACs #3 and #4 explaining why the chosen mechanism fits the existing gate design (specifically, how it relates to the fail-open asymmetric-error invariant and the existing StructuredOutputError guard), so implementers have the author's intent before making design decisions.
evidence: Summary: 'making LLM verification resilient without accepting empty outcomes' — states the outcome, not the design rationale.
Summary: 'preventing plan reviews from passing with incomplete hierarchy context' — states the outcome, not why the chosen approach fits the existing gate design.
No section of the plan body explains the design intent or why the chosen approach was selected over alternatives for ACs #3 and #4.
docs/plan-review-gate.md contains a detailed 'Asymmetric-error invariants' section that any AC#4 mechanism must engage with — the plan does not reference it.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for the chosen approach for ACs #3 and #4, which involve code changes to the plan-review gate. The Summary section describes outcomes ('making resilient,' 'preventing passes with incomplete hierarchy context') but does not state why the chosen mechanism — whatever it is — is the right fit given the existing fail-open design, the asymmetric-error invariant table, and the StructuredOutputError path. For purely documentation ACs (#1, #2, #5) the absence of rationale is benign, but for ACs that must modify a carefully-designed gate, the missing rationale leaves implementers without the author's intent.
scenarios: An implementer working on AC#4 chooses fail-closed (the obvious approach) without knowing this violates the documented asymmetric-error invariants, shipping a gate that is now stricter than intended in a systematically wrong direction.
An implementer working on AC#3 adds a zero-finding guard without knowing that zero findings is a valid outcome, introducing a false-block on clean plans.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC3: Define 'semantically empty result' precisely (e.g., verifications=[] when findings>0, or all findings grading INDETERMINATE due to zero verifications) and specify which schema field or decision rule in `plan_review_decide` / `finalize_verdict` rejects it, co-located with the schema that triggers the check.
evidence: AC3 text: 'no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome'
plan_review_verify_inputs_output.schema.json: `instructions` is a non-empty list requirement only at the call-site comment ('still emit ONE (empty) chunk'), not in the schema itself (no minItems)
plan_review_decide_input.schema.json: `verifications` is an untyped array with no minItems

workflow_ops.py plan_review_decide: 'if not pass1: return None' handles zero-findings recovery but a zero-verifications result for non-zero findings simply runs pass3_over_findings with an empty verifs dict — every finding grades to INDETERMINATE with no rejection signal
structured.py tolerant_parse: raises StructuredOutputError only on syntactically empty/unparseable output — not on a structurally valid but semantically empty verifications list
findings.finding: AC3 introduces the concept of a 'semantically empty result' that must not be accepted as a valid review outcome, but this phrase is never operationalized: no schema field enforces a minimum-items constraint on `verifications`, no decision rule in `plan_review_decide` or `finalize_verdict` distinguishes 'verifier returned zero verifications for N>0 findings' from a valid sparse result, and no co-located protocol specifies what constitutes a semantically empty outcome versus a legitimately finding-free run.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('no semantically empty result can be accepted as a valid review outcome')
scenarios: A verifier model returns `{verifications: []}` for 10 findings (e.g. because the model skips the listing under token pressure); every finding degrades to INDETERMINATE, and the review proceeds with coverage.llm_ran=True — accepted as a valid (if degraded) outcome with no distinction from a genuinely empty-findings run.
An implementer adds the rejection guard inside tolerant_parse (wrong layer) instead of at the decide/finalize boundary, missing the right contract location entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC4: Specify the schema field (e.g., a `children_load_failed` boolean on `plan_review_precheck_output` coverage or `PlanContext`) and the co-located decision rule in `finalize_verdict` that maps an incomplete-hierarchy container to INDETERMINATE rather than PASS, including the 'demonstrably irrelevant' exemption criterion (e.g., leaf ticket type or zero declared children).
evidence: AC4 text: 'Plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely, unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant'
orchestrator.py _assemble_context_uncached lines 173-178: `except Exception: logger.warning(...); listed = []` — silently continues with empty children, no structured signal
plan_review_verdict.schema.json coverage object: no 'children_load_failed' or 'hierarchy_incomplete' field defined
orchestrator.py finalize_verdict: branches on `blocking`, `llm_unavailable`, `verify_failed` — no branch for incomplete hierarchy
No schema or DET-floor check (P5 runs but only over the children it could see) inspects whether the children-enumeration itself failed
The phrase 'demonstrably irrelevant' is undefined — no rule specifies when a leaf ticket (no children expected) is exempt vs a container whose children couldn't be loaded
findings.finding: AC4 requires that plan review cannot produce a clean PASS when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely, but no schema field, coverage flag, or decision rule is specified for how an incomplete children-enumeration is detected and surfaced into finalize_verdict's PASS/INDETERMINATE branch. The current code path silently falls back to `listed=[]` and continues — no 'children_load_failed' coverage flag exists, and finalize_verdict has no branch that reads such a flag.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('Plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely')
scenarios: A container ticket's children store is temporarily unavailable; the review runs over the parent-only context, finds no G3/G4/P5 issues (since children list is empty), and produces a PASS — violating AC4 with no observable signal beyond a log warning.
An implementer adds a 'children_load_failed' flag to coverage but places it in the wrong step (e.g. assemble_criteria rather than precheck), so finalize_verdict never sees it.

evidence: Session log states: 'links: - relates_to -> 55c1-0e70-cd8d-40f5'
No mention of 55c1-0e70-cd8d-40f5 appears anywhere in the plan text.
findings.finding: The session log explicitly records a 'relates_to' link to ticket 55c1-0e70-cd8d-40f5, but the plan makes no reference to this relationship anywhere — neither in the Summary, Acceptance Criteria, nor any implied scope statement. If that ticket imposes constraints, scope boundaries, or co-requirements on this epic, the plan silently ignores them.
scenarios: Ticket 55c1-0e70-cd8d-40f5 imposes a constraint (e.g., a sequencing dependency, a shared interface contract, or an exclusion zone) that this plan's implementation violates because the plan author never consulted it.
A reviewer approving this plan assumes all stated relationships have been considered; the silent omission means any cross-ticket constraint goes unenforced until post-merge breakage is discovered.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
